dating.display: core possibly mid-1st millennium BCE; extant text redacted over centuries, stabilizing by c. 500 CE
dating.basis: Meulenbeld's survey of Indian medical literature: the compendium is a layered text with a kernel attributed to Suśruta of Vārāṇasī and later redaction (traditionally by Nāgārjuna). Single-point dates for 'Suśruta the surgeon, 600 BCE' overstate the evidence.

positions.position: Layered text, extant form by the Gupta period
Traditional date: Suśruta as a near-contemporary of the Buddha or earlier
positions.holders: Traditional accounts; often repeated in popular medicine histories
positions.evidence: Attribution and legend; reported as tradition.

summary: The surgical classic of Āyurveda: instruments, suturing, cataract couching, lithotomy, wound care, cadaver dissection for training — and pedicled-flap nasal reconstruction (nāsā-sandhāna), rebuilding a severed nose with living tissue from the cheek. Rhinoplasty had steady demand: nose-cutting was a judicial and war mutilation.
whyItMatters: This is the atlas's cleanest example of a *documented* transmission chain from an ancient practice into modern medicine — not an analogy, an actual paper trail (below). Plastic-surgery textbooks still call pedicled-forehead-flap nasal reconstruction 'the Indian method'.
honestyNote: The Suśruta Saṃhitā describes a cheek flap; the forehead-flap variant that Europe copied was documented from practitioners in Maharashtra in the 1790s — a living tradition, not a direct textual application. The chain below says exactly which hop is which.

throughLines.to: Modern reconstructive plastic surgery ('the Indian method')
chain: Suśruta Saṃhitā, Sūtrasthāna ch. 16: pedicled cheek-flap nasal reconstruction
Living Indian practice: 1793, Pune — a Maratha cart-driver, Cowasjee, mutilated in war, has his nose rebuilt with a forehead flap by a local practitioner; witnessed by East India Company surgeons
'B.L.' letter with engraving, The Gentleman's Magazine, London, October 1794 — the operation reaches European print
J.C. Carpue studies the reports for 20 years, performs Europe's first two forehead-flap rhinoplasties (published 1816)
The technique enters European surgery as 'the Indian method'; the paramedian forehead flap remains a workhorse of nasal reconstruction today

sources.label: "B.L.", letter, The Gentleman's Magazine 64 (Oct 1794), p. 891, with engraving
Carpue, J.C., An Account of Two Successful Operations for Restoring a Lost Nose (London, 1816)
Whitaker, I.S. et al., "The birth of plastic surgery: the story of nasal reconstruction from the Edwin Smith papyrus to the radial forearm flap", Plast Reconstr Surg / historical review (2007)
Bhishagratna, K.K. (tr.), An English Translation of the Sushruta Samhita, 3 vols. (Calcutta, 1907–16)
Meulenbeld, G.J., A History of Indian Medical Literature, Vol. IA (Groningen, 1999) — dating and stratification
Wujastyk, D., The Roots of Ayurveda (Penguin, 2003)

dharmicTie.statement: Āyurveda situates itself inside the Vedic corpus: the Suśruta Saṃhitā opens by declaring itself the upāṅga of the Atharvaveda, and its practice continued within dharmic institutional settings for two millennia.

scriptureRefs.text: āyurveda as upāṅga of the Atharvaveda
